comments:
Summary
This paper presents a message-passing approach for multi-agent trajectory planning based on the three-weight ADMM (TWA). The authors formulate global trajectory optimization over piecewise-linear paths, decompose the problem into small ‘‘minimizer’’ subproblems (collision, obstacle avoidance, energy/velocity constraints), derive closed-form or efficiently-solvable subroutines (including a mechanical springs-and-slab analogy for wall avoidance), and coordinate them via TWA. They show empirical results on 2D problems, compare to standard ADMM and a prior MIQP velocity-obstacle method, and report favorable scaling and run-time behavior. The paper is accompanied by supplemental material with detailed derivations and a video of trajectories.

Strengths
- The decomposition into small minimizers and use of TWA to coordinate them is clean and naturally parallelizable; this design is well suited to multi-agent planning.
- Several practical minimizers are derived explicitly (energy, max/min velocity, wall/collision), and the reduction of agent-agent collision to an agent-wall subproblem is elegant.
- The mechanical analogy for the wall minimizer provides an intuitive, implementable solver that appears efficient in practice.
- Empirical results demonstrate that TWA can significantly outperform vanilla ADMM on the tested instances and that the method can be used for both global piecewise-linear trajectories and local velocity-obstacle-style planning.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Lack of theoretical guarantees for the non-convex problem. The paper acknowledges that TWA/ADMM only have global convergence guarantees for convex problems, but there is little formal discussion about convergence behavior, robustness, or conditions under which the algorithm will (or will not) find high-quality solutions. Empirical evidence is useful but limited to a few 2D scenarios.
- Limited baselines and comparisons. The paper compares to standard ADMM and to an MIQP implementation for the VO case, but does not compare against other commonly used methods for multi-agent trajectory planning (e.g. sequential convex programming approaches, other local planners, sampling-based joint planners such as RRT*/PRM* adapted to multi-agent settings). The authors discuss some of these methods in text but empirical comparisons are missing, making it hard to judge practical advantages across a wider set of approaches.
- Experimental detail and reproducibility. Key implementation choices are reported (step-size, ρ0 scheduling), but some important details are missing or buried in appendices: exact termination criteria, tolerances, how many iterations were allowed, how parallelization was scheduled, CPU/time normalization vs external solver (CPLEX) for MIQP, and whether code will be released. The method makes ad-hoc numerical choices (e.g. substituting very small values for zero weights) whose stability properties should be discussed.
- Restriction to 2D in experiments/derivations. Although the paper indicates how to generalize, the explicit efficient solvers (mechanical analogy) are specialized to planar problems; 3D extensions are deferred to future work and may not be straightforward.
- Non-anytime behavior and safety. The algorithm is not anytime: early stopping may produce colliding trajectories and post-processing is required to extract feasible solutions. This is an important practical limitation for online or safety-critical robotics applications.

Suggestions for improvement
1. Include empirical comparisons to additional relevant methods (sequential convex programming approaches used in robotics, decentralized reactive planners, and at least one sampling-based joint planner) on representative benchmarks so readers can better gauge tradeoffs in solution quality and runtime.
2. Provide more detail on numerical stability: how are zero-weight limits implemented (choice of epsilon), sensitivity to α and ρ0, and whether different schedules affect convergence and quality.
3. Discuss and empirically characterize failure modes: when does the algorithm get stuck in poor local minima, when do deadlocks occur, and how sensitive is the method to problem conditioning or tightly constrained scenarios? Show more varied scenarios (including obstacles, heterogeneous agent radii and dynamic constraints) and statistics over many random instances.
4. If possible, release code or pseudocode plus parameter settings to enable reproducibility. Also clarify termination criteria used in experiments.
5. Provide initial steps toward 3D extensions or discuss obstacles in doing so more explicitly; readers will want to know what would be required to support aerial/3D agents.
6. Consider presenting a lightweight theoretical discussion about local convergence properties or empirical guarantees (e.g., monotone decrease of the augmented Lagrangian or conditions under which the three-weight logic aids escaping inactive constraints).

Verdict and recommendation
This paper presents a well-motivated, practical decomposition and solver that appears to offer meaningful empirical speedups over standard ADMM and can handle both global and VO-style local planning in 2D. The technical contributions (minimizers, agent-agent reduction, mechanical solver) are interesting and useful to the community. However, the paper would be stronger with broader empirical comparisons, a clearer treatment of numerical and practical limitations (including reproducibility), and more discussion on robustness/guarantees. I recommend acceptance to arXiv / presentation (score below), with encouragement to address the above points for a longer version or a journal submission.
